titleStmt.title: Morale and Home Intelligence Reports
Report: Morale Report for the period 26 March to 1 April, 1943

div.note: OFFICE OF THE CHIEF BABE CENSOR
Base Censorship Detachment NO.1
A.P.O. 700 New York, New York
1 April 1943.
Subject: Morale report for the period 26 March to 1 April, 1943, inclusive.
To: A.G. of S., G-2, War Department, Washington, D.C. (thru channels) hhh 1. Health.

Continued good health is evidenced by numerous references in letters. Remarks by patients in hospitals praising the good treatment received have been observed. The presence of lice in front line units has again been mentioned in letters. One comment complained about dysentery in camp, and another remarked about the large number of veneral cases at the hospital. About 95% of all comments on health have been favorable. The following are excerpts from letters examined:

"These army doctors are terrific. They function even better than civilians. I think I changed my mind about social medicine. I will vote for it after the war is over."--17th F.A. (Patient at the 7th Station Hosp.)
"I have gained about 15 pounds since I got over here and can faithfully say that the American army is the best fed and clothed army in the world."-­9th Bn., 1st Replacement Depot.
"We have 550 clap patients already. So Marko be a good boy, don't mess around with any women."--2nd Convalescent Hospital, DS, 180th Station Hosp.
"Can't hardly wash or shave, it's not like the States all you want of everything. The lice do everything but carry you away, that's war life."-- Hq. & Hq. Co., 1st Bn, 26th Infantry.
"Most of our camp came down with disentsry....I was no exception.... but mine only lasted for a day It was no fun "--9th Bn., 1st Replacement Depot. hhh 2. Mail.

It is estimated that 70% of all comments about the mail service are unfavorable. Numerous complaints have been observed about not having received any mail for long periods and in the event that a large volume of mail has been received by units the complaint is about the irregularity. Articles published in the States praising the mail service to this theatre have been severely criticised. The following comments were received:
"We have had no mail now for a long strech. We certainly miss it, as you can expect."--Hq. 2nd Bn., 30th Infantry.

"I've got nothing to complain of here so far except my mail which is not coming in as it should be. Poor mother apparently still does not know that I safely arrived in North Africa. After 6 weeks this makes me very angry at the P.O."--57th Signal Bn.

"I have been looking for a letter from you and Daisy for over a month and haven't received one yet. I don't know whether it's a delay in the mail or not but I like to hear from you and her once in a while so that I will know that I am not forgotten."--Co. E, 92nd Engr. Regt.

"I've just been censoring the mail the boys of my detachment send home and they all have one thing in common; that constant hunger for familiar faces, family, home, and what makes it tougher is the fact that we haven't had any mail for over a month."--505th C.A. SL AA.
"You said you hadn't received a letter from me in 3 months. All I can do is write and if you can't receive them I can't help it for I write you every week."--Company D, 79 Ordnance.
"Got a whole bunch of letters today, mostly all January issue though. That's the way it comes in bunches like bananas. The last first the first last."--Battery B, 105th C.A. (AA).

"I received 2 letters from you a couple of days ago. One was mailed Jan. 18 and the other was P.M. Feb. 5th. That just goes to show what our mail service is like. At first we had hopes that the situation would improve but we have given up all hope now. As a matter of fact I believe it takes longer for a letter to reach us non than it did a few months back."-­345th Fighter Squadron, 350th Fighter Group.

"Mail seems to have stopped arriving for some unknown reason. It seems a better system of handling mail to us could be worked out. Next to food it's our most important item and even food doesn't mean much after several weeks of no mail. Why do they send ever 2nd Lt. over here by plane. They run around here several weeks without any assignment and it would be much more important to lead the planes with mail instead of with officers just coming from 0.C. school."--2nd Service Area Command.

"There is sure a lot of room for the improvement of the mail situation. It's a joke. I read some of the articles that were written about the wonder­ful service we are getting in Africa. I think if more time were spent trying to do something to improve the situation instead of wasting time trying to fool people about how efficient the system is, that we would be able to receive mail a lot sooner. Almost all the mail coming over here is at least a month old. Then they write stories about mail being delivered a few hours after we came in here. It is bad enough to have to wait that long for mail; but then to read about the quick delivery we receive over here sure is a joke."--21st Signal Co. Service Group.

"It was announced over here that mail destined for here was lost. Since reading that article in the 'Readers Digest' about the Army Postal Service, I am receiving facetious inquiries as to whether we have sent divers down to get it. While he was about it, Mr. High might have come over here for material for his article. Some of his inferences--if not his statements were ridiculous. I wonder if he was kidded or was he trying to kid some-body."--Hq., VI Corps. hhh 3. Training.

Comments on this subject criticise the training being conducted here and the apparent lack of training of newly arrived personnel. About 90% of comments observed have been of a critical nature. Note the following:

"We received several new 2nd Lts. from the States. They are worse than recruits coming into combat area. Have to be taught by enlisted men--plane indentification, what to do when being strafed, what to do when being bombed, and many other things. They would not live long if the experienced enlisted men did not take care of them bodily and then they throw their rank around when not in danger."--Hq. Battery, 175th Field Artillery.

"We have a training schedule that calls for setting up offices in the field and getting used to conditions as experienced on the front lines. Forced marches through the night. 36 hours without slepp, limited water and food and building and breaking down tents at a few minutes notice. These various training details makes an outfit like ours into builders, movers, soldiers, and every other imaginable thing in the army."--Hq. VI Corps.

"Aoy, whatever you do don't get shipped out of that place. I am in the most screwed outfit you can imagine. It was bad enough back at Kilmer and has steadily gotten worse until now it is at the bottom. I have been sitting on my fanny here in N. Africa for five weeks teaching close order drill, rifle marksmanship and so forth to every branch of service. They don't and I dot have a bit of interest in any of it, and still no non-coms. What I wouldn't give to get into a regular outfit."--9th Bn., 1st Replace. Depot.

"I have put in for a transfer for the far east as I am tired of doing nothing beside run a school which has no business to exist. If they do not know anything at this stage of the war it seems to me too late to learn and if they cannot be trained in the States what am I supposed to do with them. I am sure I cannot perform miracles."--lst Amphibian Brigade.
"re have moved to a rear area and are going through a process of training the Air Corps in flying and jumping parachute troops."--2nd Bn., 509th Parachute Infantry. hhh 4. Equipment.

About 95% of the references to equipment have been favorable. The P-38 and B-26 planes have been praised while the P-39 plane has been mentioned as being inferior to the enemy planes. The effectiveness of the 105 MI, mounted on half tracks, has been favorably commented on. In general, it is evident that the troops have confidence in their equipment. The following are comments observed during the past week:
"Don't let anyone tell you that the American equipment isn't good. After seeing the other armies stuff and also seeing how ours stand up over here it is OK for me."--19th Replacement Depot.

"The 105 MM on half tracks are really can openers for these German tanks even up to the Mark VI's, which are damn good machines."--439th Bombardment Squadron. "Vie have growing confidence in our B-26B type aircraft. Have been having a big talk and whatnot with veterans and they report our airplane stays together when, has no right to. The boys have been getting back." --442nd Bomb. Sq., 320 Bomb. Group. "All the news that we have gotten as to the P-38's fighting qualities has been very good. We've seen quite a few P-39's flying around here but not one P-38. They say that the P-39's don't even fight with the Germans. They're not good enough. The P-38's fight with them though, and they are better than the German ships too. The P-38's are all up at the front. None way back here. Too valuable."--19th Replacement Bn.

"The German anti-aircraft is very effective and is the main fear of our air force. The 190 is also to be considered as well as the new 109 with the 190 having a slight edge. The Italian flyers are also beginning to get pretty hot but there are not so many of them. The P-38 is not all they had hoped it would be but it is still by far the best we have with the exception of the Spitfire "--19th Replacement Bn. 1st Repl. Depot. hhh 5. Food.

During the past week about 98% of the comments on food have beer favorable. Recent issue of fresh meat to units has been enthusiastically received and mentioned very frequently. Unfavorable comments mentioned the shipment of oranges to this theater from the States, and criticised the English ration. The following are typical comments:

"From some source or other there as come to us of late a supply of fresh meat, so we've been eating unusually well. I'm not too optimistic that it will continue thus, but it's nice while it lasts. We've had chicken, veal and beef, probably in better supply than most of you are able to get it now."--Hq., VI Corps.

"As you can see by the address we are under American Command. Call me Yank. The Americans supply us with everything except clothes and pay. Still they feed us very well. We think nothing of getting eggs and bacon for breakfast tinned tomatoes or pork chops. One day we actually got turkey for dinner, tinned fruit (pears, peaches, cherries and fruit cock­tail) we get too much of, chocolate, sweets, oranges and apples are not scaree."--822 Sm Coy, Pioneer Corps, A.P.O. 600 (British Soldier).

"I think that is was very silly of that woman to tell mother that the boys here are dying from hunger and malaria. There isn't any army in the world that gives the boys the food the American boys get. We get food and plenty of it. Our drinking water is• well tested and always fixed with chlorine so it's safe to drink. We have been here over a month and there hasn't been any illness yet, except a few colds."--Hq.Co. VI Corps.

"We are getting good food, yesterday chicken for dinner, this morning two eggs for breakfast, with a cereal and bread and marmalade, coffee. Tea is always a hot meal, have never had such grub in the army before, always have canned fruits after dinner and tea. It's always coffee we get to drink, never tea."--3 Section (SM) Pioneer Corps, A.P.O. 600. (British Soldier).

"Cur food has been rather good ever since we landed here and lately it is improving somewhat. We have had chicken a few times and fresh beef, also eggs a few times though they are rather scarce. We do not lack quantity, only the variety, but there is no complaint on this score."-­Battery G, 67th C.A. (AA).

"You should have been in our mess hall yesterday for lunch. After about 100 had gone through, some ice cream arrived and the effect was terrific, the boys were like kids slipping back into the line for a 2nd 3rd and even 4th serving."--14th Fighter Group, 37th Fighter Squadron.

"We are certainly a well fed and cared for army living better than any of the civilians have ever lived here. So you can see that all of your rationing is accomplishing a purpose after all. I am amazed at the great quantities of supplies of all sorts that they have been able to bring over here. Most people don't realize the amount of supplies that it takes to keep an army in the field, and therefore expect too much in to short a time." --17th Bomb. Squadron, 27th Aomb. Group.

"We had a pleasant surprise last Sunday. For the 1st time in almost 8 months we had ice cream and some Coca Cola. Our food over here has been very good. 7e were issued 60 chickens for our company."--Company C, 240th Q.M. Bn. (Serv).
"We finally went on American rations last week and what a difference they make. The morale of the men was increased 1005 and that is our hardest job.!--Company C, 63rd Signal Battalion.
"We get more rations of that kind in a week over here than got in England in a month."--Company A, 805th T.D.
"We had fresh meat once last week and did it ever taste good after Spam for weeks."--15th Evacuation Hospital.

"We had a fine chicken last night and today at dinner. We had sweet potatoes and string beans and white-potatoes and biscuits and coffee. I'll eat us out of house and home if I have this same appetite when I get horse that I have now ."--''Med. Det., 92nd Engineer Regiment.
"I just had a big steak dinner and yesterday we had chicken, I can hardly believe it, it certainly is a thrill to get some food like that for once...."--8th Replacement Depot,

"Gosh, honey we here feel sorry for you people at home... I had steak in the mess kitchen today...also we have fresh meat every day, so you can see that we are getting things over here for the boys to eat..."--8th Bn., 1st Replacement Depot. "You will rem ember that in a previous letter you asked me about the food and I was ashamed to answer for awhile, hut now I will tell you the food is great and plenty of it too...chicken, roast beef, ham, and plenty of coffee. I'm kinda worried about the food they may be fattening us for the kill....It's like getting anything you want before you walk the last mile."--62nd C.A. (AA).

"I certainly take off my hat to Uncle Sam for the fine manner in which he is caring for his boys overhere, perhaps the rationing program back in the States has made all this possible--at least it is a big help and I want all of you to know that you haven't been doing without certain items in vain."--325th Fighter Group.

"It is incredible the amount of grub the Yankees give out as rations. Well, dear honest I feel down right ashamed to eat such luxuries knowing how hard things are at home."--822 SM Co. Pioneer Corps, A.P.O. 600 (Br. Soldier).

"We had chicken today for dinner and supper, 2 times in one day. For dinner we had stewed chicken and for supper we had fried chicken. To think we are in Africa and getting chicken at this time of the year."--470th Engineers.

"We have a new mess officer I'm glad to ay and are now enjoying very good food. Last night we had steak and tonight ham and we have had fresh eggs the last two mornings. The steak I had last night was the third piece of fresh meat I've had in two months and it surely did taste good."-­Base Hq., Transporation Section, A.P.O. 600.

"We sure have been getting some swell meals lately though. Believe it or not I have had steak twice this week. Just living like kings. I sort of feel sorry for the folks back home the way they are rationed."--2nd Medical Supply Depot.

"We are getting enough to eat so don't worry about me not having enough to eat. We have the best of what there is to offer of fresh meats, chickens and so forth. We had chicken one day and steak the following two days. Aut mostly we get canned food."--Hq.VI Corps.

"Things haven't been too bad over here. The food has been excellent, is in fact too good that I am on a diet so I won't get sugar diabetic. Have to lose about 20 pounds."--1991st Q (Trk-Avn), 41st Service Group.

"At present we are bivouaced in pup tents and eat "c" rations. In the chow line we get a cup of hot water and two small cans, one containing meat and beans or vegetable hash and the other containing five hard concen­trated biscuits, a little can of soluble coffee, three lumps of sugar and about 5 pieces of hard concentrated candy. I find it entirely a wholesome and satisfactory meal."--Company M, 301st Ordnance Regiment.

"I haven't been eating anything lately except tea and crackers. We have tea for every meal. I have to smoke English cigarettes now as we don't get any American ones. They talk about how much the American people give up for the boys overseas, well I have never seen any of it and most of the other guys over here haven't either. Most of the things we get are English and I'm beginning to think that if it was not for them we would not exist now."--Hq.. Battery, 107th C.A. Bn.

"The food is as lousy as ever--the navy right down te street eats like kings. Oh yes, guess what arrived from the States--nice big juicy sunkist California oranges--of all things--sending that stuff over here is like 'carrying coal to Newcastle', just taking up room that could be better used for a nice batch of beef (that's a strange word ) I wonder what it looks like anymore."--361st Quartermaster An.
"Your rationing situation is getting awfully tough; think I'll stay on here for'we have everything but meat and I'll be blamed if I know where it's going."--Hq. XII AFSC. hhh 6. Entertainment.

Entertainment facilities provided by the Red Cross have been well received by he troops. It has been noted that the U.S.O. has been the subject of increasing criticism for their failure to provide any services for overseas troops. The following are comments on this subject:

"The Red Cross is very active here, they have a big theatre where free movies are shown. Then they have an officers club and an Allied Club. All in all they have four large buildings. They also sell the only newspaper written in English which is available to us."--534th Army Postal Unit.

"I do want to mention the 'New Red Cross Service Club', not far away. Thanks to 'Uncle Sam', as he tries to give his boys overseas the best. Don't you think they deserve it too? I see a movie most every week there, also many games as ping pang, checkers, etc. and they are a real help. Remember when you give your donation to the Red Cross you may be helping out a friend far away and we appreciate it very much."--6th Chemical Co. (Depot).

"The U.S.O. ought to put out portable libraries for the boys or folding tables and chairs and a tent that light wouldn't shine through. What the hell am I talking about. The U.S.O. never does anything practicle it's only reason for existance is graft and for those poor dear boys back in the States who haven't been able to go home for six whole months and who get tired of army cooking. The U.S.O. is the laughing stock of the army. I have never heard any army man say a good ward for it. Of course we who have been overseas for over a year have never seen any of it except in Life Magazine. Talking about food, we have had stew, hard tack and cold corned beef for exactly 41 days straight."--Co. A, 109th Engr. An. hhh 7. Sports.

The efforts of the units to provide sports programs for the men are well received and appreciated by the men. Favorite sports are volley ball, soft ball, basket ball and swimming. Aasket ball competition between U.S. Army teams and the French North African team has also created considerable interest. The lack of time off in some units to participate in sports has been mentioned. The following are typical comments:

"Been doing a little spring training, we're going to have a soft ball league and we're in it. Boy, I hope we have the good luck we had in England. We have the same team, so should come out 0.K."--339th Service Sq.

"Late this afternoon we have another volley ball game. Hq. Aattery here played the second platoon and we won three out of four. We came by the picture show on the way back to camp. We didn't see all of the picture but it was pretty good."--Battery A, 103rd C.A.

"I noticed on my travels that there is no lack of athletic stadiums throughout all of N.Africa. This I believe gives evidence that there is a certain amount of recreation carried on over here in peacetime. I think we should be able to set up a large sport program which would be of great benefit for our troops. I think there will be a lot of games carried on after the American troops leave this theater and the war is over, The natives are all very interested in our various games and like basketball about the best."--North African Air Force.

"We had a basketball game between the 3rd Port and the French North African team and we won it by one point 24-23. Our team played them once before on their own court and really got beat. We like very much to play with the French it is always lot of excitement."--3rd Port,T.C.

"Each evening between dinner and darkness the officers get out and play volleyball. The boys are beginning to ask for time for going swimming and we will have to give them at least apart of an afternoon off. I think it is very important that the men go swimming as much as possible."--261st Med. Amph. An.
"I have been able to do a little swimming lately which is very relaxing and refreshing."--Transportation Section, Base Headquarters, A.P.O. 600. hhh 8. Leave.

Several favorable comments have been observed about the beneficial effects of the rest camp established for air corps personnel. Other comments on this subject usually complain about the absence of leaves for long periods in it is theater. About 90% of the comments from ground forces are un­favorable. The following have been observed in mail examined:

"We have our own rest camp on the shores of the Mediterranean and I have been down for my first official three days rest. I am burned like a lobster and it sure feels good. It sure was a pleasure to get rest for the first time in ages."--60th Troop Carrier Group.

"Last night when I got back from the rest camp marked the end of the most pleasant three days that I have had oversee. I didn't really realize how sick and tired I had gotten of this same old daily routine until I got away from it. After living in our hot desert the swimming and soaking in the beautiful Mediterranean gave me back my energy and my physical vigor." --60th Troop Carrier Group.

"You speak of fellows being away for over two years and not receiving a furlough. That rather amuses me for this reason. I spent six months in China, two and a half years in Hawaii, two years in Panama and then they sent me to the U.S.A. to become an officer. Me expecting to remain in the U.S. for a while at least-but what happens-a month after they donate me a pair of bars they ship me to Ireland so in all that time I have not received so much as a day's leave-that is to get home. I've had some time off at different OD & R depots but no chance to drop out to Portland, Oregon and give the folks the hello. I'm not griping but some of these abba-dabbas give me the best of laughs."--Hq. & Hq. 1st Ranger Bn. hhh 9. Quarters.

There have been no serious complaints about quarters, and the majority of the comments refer with pride to the improvements that the writers have been able to accomplish through their own efforts. Several references to leaky pup tents have been observed. The following comments are typical:
"We are out in the field now. I wish you see my little home I have built. It is about a 6 x 6 dug down in the ground 3 feet deep with a good cover on it."--6th Chemical Company Depot.

"For awhile when we first came here, the ground was our mattress and we lived in pup tents. Now we have straw mattresses and we live inside once again. I doubt if I could sleep in a soft bed again becuase for a spring, I have planks of woo and a straw mattress to soften it up a bit. I don't mind it much but it certainly does not get any softer with each succeeding day but nevertheless I manage to sleep. For a pillow I use most anything, pants, shirt and my field jacket. I make the best of everything because there is no point in grumbling. We washed out of anything that would hold water, tin cans, helmets, etc. The army installed an outdoor shower and so we were able to bathe but with salt water, it is most diffi­cult to lather with the soap. That is why most soldiers have their hair cut short. Now we have running water in our camp. The camp now is an old French barracks."--261st Medical Battalion, Company A.

"We found our new home and am still at the same location with a class 'b' camp after many weeks of hard labor. It is one of my duties to build things to make it more comfortable for us to live. We have two French fellows who are handy with a hammer and saw, so we have most of our furni­ture built, two tier bunks, chairs, benches, writing tables, mess tables, a ping pong table, clothes racks, etc."--6th Chemical. Co. (Depot)

"There is a limited amount of large tents so we are in our own pup tents. I don't mind them at all as I have lived in them before, but there are many officers here who have never lived in them and they are really sore. It won't do them any good in the field and they better Iearn to get used to this kind of life."--lst Bn. 1st Replacement Depot.

When we came here first, we spent the first couple of weeks in pup tents and it was anything but pleasant. It rained upon our arrival, and we had to set up the tents in the rain and sleep in the mud. It really was more than miserable because our pup tents are absolutely not waterproof. However, conditions for us improved in the past days, we have moved into large pyramidals and it now possible to sit up and stay dry while it is raining. On the whole it is not too intolerable and I like the present setup."--18th Replacement An., 1st Replacement Depot.

"I don't know if you know about my new change of scenery or not so I'll tell you a bit about it. Well, I'm in action and it's not too much fun. For one thing we have to practically live in holes. Even now, I'm in a hole about five feet deep and as square. We have two pup tents pitched together and Ellis and I sleep in one end and have our message center in the other. I'm very good at jumping in my fox hole in nothing flat. New records are being set up all the time so it keeps us busy keeping up with the rest of the outfits. That's about all I can tell you about our activities, the rest you'll have to imagine."--Hq. Battery, 151st F.A. Bn.

"The living conditions for soldiers out here are awful. Here I sit in a driving rain under a leaking pup tent in a puddle of mud, dirty, gooey, cold and wet. If people back home realized this more they would be damn thankful they are back in the U.S."--lst Replacement Depot.

"Yesterday I really bettered my lying conditions. I changed my billet from the hotel to a private apartment. The government requisitioned it for me and is not costing me anything. My main reason for moving was due to the fact that the hotel owner wanted to double up and triple the number of officers in each room. This fact coupled with the fact that I was so distant from my warehouse, made it logical for me to move."--QM Section, Base Headquarters, APO 600. hhh 10. Politics.

Comments have been observed approving General Giraud's policy of cancelling the laws and restrictions imposed by Vichy and relate that the beneficial effects of his action are already apparent. An increasing number of references are being observed about the possibility of the spread of communism after the war and express the thought that it may be a solution of the European situation. The following were extracted from letters examined:

"Within the past two weeks, since General Giraud's speech (lifting the ban on Jews in North Africa) numerous stores have opened up, business is humming and everything has taken on a more prosperous nature."--6th General Disp.

"The political situation in North Africa is gradually straightened up too and that was enlightening to learn why there was so much 'silk glove' diplomacy. We all hope that when we will invade the continent that we will have the right men with us who know something about the political situation in the respective countries and that we will be able to do a better job than we did here in North Africa."--7th Squadron, 62nd T.C. Group.

"You disturb me a little with your remarks about being afraid of what Russia will do after the war. I am afraid you are coming under the influence of a reactionary press, or Church, or something. From this angle, it looks like we've a lot more to fear, after the war, and now also, from the react­ionaries and fascists and appeasers in our own government, than from the Russians. I should say if the Russians went to crack off Berlin, let them. Who has a better right. And as far the fear that Russia will make Europe communistic, it might not be a bad idea. Certainly Captialism has not made anything very peaceful out of Europe, and certainly the Church has not made anything very holy out of Europe. If communism can build a state in which folk can live peacefully, and with enough, and with the minimum freedoms of speech and religions then let them try it. That will be better than having short periods of greater freedom broken up and interspersed with periods of war and dictatorship such as has been the period of Europe for more years than we can remember. I will grant you that I don't wish to live in a communistic state, and I don't think anyone should have to unless the majority want it."-- hhh 11. News and Propaganda.

Comments on recent news expressed approval of Prime Minister Churchill's plans for the conduct of the war and post war policy. One writer criticised articles written by war correspondents about the North African campaign for creating wrong impressions about the hardships of the soldiers. Lack of news has been complained of. The publishing and distribution of the Signal Corps News Bulletin is appreciated, but evidently does not reach all units. Methods used by the enemy for propaganda to the troops are obviously providing entertainment and have the effect of raising morale. Some concern has been expressed about false reports of casualties being sent to families of troops. The following are comments on news and propaganda:

"And now for a lecture young lady--I've told you repeatedly not to take such stock in the news commentators. It is my candid opinion that everyone at home would be better off if Raymond Clapper, Ernie Pyle and their like were not allowed at the front. The soldiers here are doing a job--and a darned good one, and they don't expect the folks back home to sit up worrying about them. They are doing it because it is their job and are paying a debt to the country. We are not heroes but just guys trying to do a good job and get it over with so we can all go back home to our old jobs and the ones we love. So darling, for heavens sake quit worrying about the war news from North Africa."--Signal Section, Aase Hq., APO 600.

"What was your opinion of Churchill's speech the other night. I feel that he was expressing not only the hope of Europeans and the Empire but also those 130 millions Americans. When he spoke of partial demobilization of forces after the fall of Germany I disagreed with him heartily. However I want to come home I am opposed to any demobilization until the Japanese Empire has first been crushed. The international Police system, four year reconstruction period and social security are absolutely necessary if we are to prevent the same chaos that followed the last war. Russia will and should have a powerful influence on the peace. From her progressive social security program feasible features will be adopted by the nations both victorious and defeated. All of the Soviet government ideas are not screwy. we've learned a lot from these allies and will learn more."--156th Inf.

"I just listened to the news from London and it sounded pretty good. We may get this thing over with one of these days and get back to the States, I hope. We heard Ohurchill's speech last night and it has been rebroadcast several times today. We also heard a transcribed command performance last night that was made in the States and rebroadcast from England."--81st Ftr. Gp. Hq. Detach.

"There is the customary excitement around here. The high spot of the day is always the reading of the Signal bulletin over on the bulletin board telling what other troops are doing. Otherwise we don't get any news at all."--Engineers Section, Base Headquarters, A.P.O. 600.

"Met fellows from an outfit I used to be in as soon as we reached our area they were as surprised to see me as I was to see them. Believe me, fellows, those guys were hungry for any news from the States--they swamped me with questions about everything that makes news. Even their knowledge of the progress of our scrap here was based on rumor--they apparently never see a newspaper nor get near a radio. I can just see myself two or three months from now if our information is as meager as that which they get."-­73rd Station Hospital.

"The Germans are sure pouring the propaganda at us over here. We have short wave set and pick up a program we call Sally of the Axis. It's just typical Aerlin balony. You poor sons at home are starving to death according to them. We just can't swallow this crap. We all hope each night that we would hear a bomb whistle over the radio and then have it go off the air. Then we'd know that they have gone where they belong."--Hq. 325th Fighter Group.

"The Germans seem to reverse their propaganda tactics. They admit very frequently that heavy damage and large casualties were afflicted by the RAF raids. This method of their propaganda is quite new and I wonder whether they don't want to fool us by giving us informations which easily could make us overoptimistic."--325th Fighter Group.

"I heard a German propaganda broadcast tonight. Imagine the dopes trying to have us believe that our country doesn't know how to do things. Saying that there is no coffee in the U.S. The programs are very entertaining to us. In fact they boost our morale. I wonder what they would give for a nice cup of coffee on the Russian front or anywhere in Germany. I don't think they have any coffee. The Super race they call themselves. I say they're not the super race but the Super Super dopes."--Hq., 325th Ftr.Gp.

"'While I'm thinking of it we heard something over here about German agents in the U.S.A. who have been writing letters to parents who have sons over here telling them their sons have been killed in action. Remember and impress upon the folks that should anything happen to me, you will be notified officially by the War Department. That sounds pretty morbid, but you and I realize what would happen should they receive a letter and believe it."--443rd Squadron, 320th Group. hhh 12. Church and religion.

It is evident from comments received that a large number of troops are again attending church and turning to the bible, after having neglected them before coming into the combat zone. It is evident that some Commanding Officers are encouraging attendance at church and this fact is appreciated by the men. The following are comments received:

"I was reading in the new testiment about you and I and I am sending you the Chapter 6 & 7 of 1 Cornithians and I did not know there was so much peat in it for a weary soul as there are. I am not sending it to you as good as saying you are guilty of doing these things but them are a great consolation when you get restless and weary. I think you understand what I mean."--Company A, 28th Quartermaster Regiment.

"We have had two church services here in the field and there certainly was a gain in attendance from what it used to be. I make much use of my Bible and there are truly comforting words found there. I was reading some Psalms the other day. I was reading the 94th Psalm and I surely like that one. I memorized some of the verses such as the 19th verse and more of them. I will say this, that my Bible is the greatest morale builder that there is."--Ha. Battery, 151st F.A. An.

"I am going to church every morning that I can during the season of lent. We have a Catholic Church about one minute from my barracks so I have no excuse not to go unless I am on duty or away."--Hq. VI Corps.

"Here is one thing that might make you feel a little happy; one of the Colonels here put himself out to stop me and gave me his most profound congratulations on my going to church every day. I thought that it was rather nice of him to go out of his way to tell me."--Hq. VI Corps.

"For the first time in many moons I find myself going to church. I have found out that I have neglected my inner soul too long. I sat through the sermon feeling as though the preacher was pointing me out as the lone sinner of the congregation--it felt good listening to the pastor. He held us spell-bound. I learned also to my amazement that I no longer know the songs that they sing. It is not my desire to become a religious fanatic but I do want religion to occupy some part of my daily efforts. I intend to spend Easter morning in Church this year (Remember how we both always planned to spend Easter and Christmas in Church and yet never did). Please go this year and maybe our thoughts can span the oceans and unit us together. We can think back to that day when we stood side by side and made out vows that made us man and wife."--Co. D, 531st Engr. Shore Regiment. hhh 13 Home affairs.

Comments have expressed disappointment with the war effort at home and severly criticise strikes in war industries. One writer suggested that an effort be made to enlist the aid of all refugees in South America to relieve the shortage of manpower in the United States. The following are comments on home affairs:

"I was reading March 1, Time Magazine and it made me very upset to learn of the petty bickering going on in the nation among our politicians. People back in the States ought to live as we do and eat the same as we do and they might realize how it is to be a soldier in the field. They are still too comfortable there is they have time to argue about politics and to strike."--57th Signal Battalion.

"Every now and then I read about Unions and strikes. So help me God, I know what I'd like to do to any bird who would strike on any war industry. Shooting is too good. They shoot spies, but at least a spy is prompted by feeling for his own country--nobody but a fool would deny that a man has certain amount of something to risk his neck for his counrtry--but these strikers are merely selfish."--804th T.D. Bn.
"If the people in the States could get a sample of life over here, I doubt if you would have all the strikes and griping that you now have."--A.P.O. 4015.

"We hear very much about shortage of manpower in the States and we understand that the army also has quite some headaches to fill its quota. As this war is a global war why don't we invite all free people over the whole world to help us in this struggle. In South America for instance are hundred thousands of former European people who left their countries after Hitler took them over. Why don't we give to those people a chance to fight with us against their mortal enemy. Why don't we invite all those Dutch, Polish, Belgian, Norwegian, Czech, Jugoslav and Greek fellows to join us in this crusade. I am quite sure that thousands and thousand would be eager to answer our call whether they are in Chile, Bolivia, Argentine or Venezuela. And that Foreign Legion certainly would have the real fighting spirit and they would pay back to Mr. Schickelgruber for all the crimes he committed to them. We always talk about a war of the United Nations. So let us forget about all the red tape and let us mobilize the people of the United Nations wherever they might be."--427th Engineers.

"A paper from Newark, N.J.and it had a lot of stuff about rationing at home, that has started me to worry about you and if you and the children are getting enough to eat. From what the paper said, if it were all true, things at home are bad and it makes me very unhappy to think you are suffer­ing from the war. There's no kind of hardship that I wouldn't go through if it would make it easier for you and the children."--Administrative Section, 2nd Provisional Prisoner of War.

"I certainly hope that whoever plans the peace which will eventually come, will have enough sense and vision to at least prevent war again for long time. Wars seem inevitable but at least they can be fewer and less severe just as science and medicine can diminish disease."--Tran. Section, Aase Headquarters, A.P.O. 600. hhh 14. Relations with civilian population.

About 60 of comments on this subject are unfavorable, due mostly to the fact that soldiers are charged exhorbitant prices. Some writers remark that a good part of this is due to the soldier himself for throwing money around when they first arrived. Instances where troops have been invited to French homes are numerous and invariably these comments remark about the excellent treatment received. It has been suggested by one writer, that medical service be provided for the civilian population as a means of improving better relations. The following are typical comments:

"The biggest item of expense out here is laundry. I have located a Spanish family that does my laundry, my monthly bill running easily to $8,00. Food prices are so high that soldiers when on pass eat at local Red Cross Clubs and seldom patronize French restaurants."--345 Ftr. Sq. 350th Ftr. Gp.

"It is getting to be the consensus of opinion that the American soldier is the biggest sucker in the world. They pay whatever price is asked for anything. Wine here when we first got here was selling for four to ten francs a quart and now we are paying 100 or even 150 for the same thing. Some of the boys are paying 6 or 7 dollars for laundry and before the change of the Franc they were getting it done for about a dollar."--60th T.C. Group.

"If anyone asks you how to make permanent friends out of these people tell them to send over cases of chewing gum. The soldiers have done more for international harmony with a package of gum than all the speeches any­one could make. That's one language everyone understands. Another mistaken idea is that Mohamedans hate Christians. I have several Mohamedan friends."-­2644 G.R. Depot Q.M.

"One of my duties is to censor mail, and in one letter, evidently written by a Jewish boy, he comments that he was invited to the Rabbi's house for a Purim dinner. He noticed that the Rabbi had on only one sock, and the only food was a few oranges, some small meatballs, and a little wine. So he didn't eat anything but the oranges. It's the same everwhere you go. I was in Oran not so long ago. Before the war it was a city of 200,000; today it is crowded with 500,000 persons. There is very little food there. However, American ships are bringing in food and flour, and the people look well and healthy. Although the French have begun charging the American soldiers high prices, the boys are getting even by selling some of their clothing at equally high prices. One kid sold an Arab one sheet for about a thousand francs, and any Arab would sell his life to get hold a pair of heavy army shoes. One of the fellows gave the Arabian chief of police in our village a pair of socks, and there is nothing he wouldn't do for us now."--19th Replacement Bn. A.P.O. 700. "I doubt whether anything would win as much friendship at so little cost as arranging a good medical service for the native population. Yesterday morning an Arab came into camp groaning and waving his hands in excited despair. His mother was very ill, would die before noon unless a doctor saw her. So permission secured from local authorities I went there. She had a four day old pneumonia. I hope that with injections I will be able to rescue her. You should have seen how these people place naive faith in the doctor and their joyous demonstration for the help I gave them." --261st Medical Amph. An.

"When we landed the rate of exchange was so high that we paid no attention to the cost of anything but since the franc has gone up and prices are beyond reason we are gradually realizing that prices are high as hell even with things back in the States. Pipes that I paid the equivalent of fifty cents for several weeks back are now five dollars for the same ones. The American troops certainly played hell with their money here."--439th Bomb. Sq. hhh 15. Relations with Allied troops.

Mutual admiration of each other has been expressed by both American and British soldiers and relations between these two are most friendly. The success of the Aritish 8th Army has caused a revision of opinion in the minds of the American soldier about the fighting ability of the British soldier. No comments have been observed dealing with our relations with the French troops. All comments received were favorable. Note the following:
"I am now working with Americans. They are a grand lot. Many of them have visited England and they never cease to talk of the welcome they received from our folks."--Pilot Officer, British.

"The Yanks continue to be awfully good to me; their generosity is beyond competition. Yesterday one gave me a pound of tobacco, about six weeks supply, another gave me six Havana cigars; it is most embarrassing. There is a local wine to be obtained and usually the officers subscribe together to buy a cask. There is no question of my contributing--they were offended when I offered."--5th Army Engr. Training Center.(Br.Officer).

"I am very glad to inform you that the relations with our British Allies have become much better since we left England. certainly have to admit that the British VIII Army is doing a magnificent job and that they are darn good soldiers. Also our men respect the Aritish very much and you don't hear anymore those derogatory remarks 'about strategic retreats' and all the bragging how good the Yanks are and how bad the (limeys' were. I hope these good relations will improve after the Tunisian campaign will be finished and the Yanks and the Tommies will get ready for the European campaign."--C.W. Section, Aase Headquarters, A.P.O. 600.

"When we were in England our men felt quite superior about the British. I must admit that they have changed their mind quite a bit since we have been in Africa. They remember very often the time they spent in England and I hear often a remark about 'when we were in England'. As a unit censor I see how much mail is going to friends in Ireland and England."--2624th Signal Service Regiment. hhh 16. Censorship.

Illicit posting of mail was again the chief violation of censorship during the past week. Of a total of 29 Forms 912 sent out, 10 were for illicit posting. Another frequent violation during the past week was by officers enclosing classified official documents and maps in foot lockers being sent to the States by parcel post. Other violations included: linking an A.P.O. with a geographical location; location of organization; use of improper address; details of voyage; details of journey by air; careless unit censorship; information a concerning naval forces and equipment and unauthenticated or false reports, misleading statements and unconfirmed rumors. hhh 17. General complaints.

While lack of promotion is the subject of many officers' complaints, complaints of enlisted men pertain mostly to non-receipt of allotments by dependants, and failure of their families to receive safe arrival cards. The following are some of the comments that have been noted:

"Have been prodding the Col. for a different assignment and even though I am actually surplus on the T/0 I can't seem to get out of this place--no, not that I'm indispensable; far from that, but as always in military circles a C.O. wants to have as many men under him as possible-and though our chief nabob here has said that our T/O has to be cut almost in half, the Col. is bucking it and constantly writing memos on why we should have so and so many officers rather than the reduced number, which, I might add is sufficient for anything we're trying to do they. But still persist, etereoistc:szareiotgong way in the end anyhow."--CWS, Aase Headquarters, A.P.O. 600.

"With all my preparation, study and time spent I figured I'd be at least a major when the war finally broke out-but the fates conspired against me. Now I have the bitter disappointment of seeing men from civilian life or with little or no training and experience skyrocket past me and it's an empty satisfaction to know that I least I am capable of handling any job they give me. But from what I gather almost anyone can get a commission these days. Of course I don't know how they do it. Do you have to get a letter from Hague-or does one enlist and then demand a commission? Some of the officers I have seen must have gotten their commission by mail order. Some of the products of training and leadership to these officers fill our Detention Camp so fast that we can hardly release patrolees fast enough to accommodate the new ones."--2615 MPDTC.

"To top things off, new officers with recent promotions are coming in and taking over choice spots above us poor suckers, who have been away from training too long to know the latest developments. Aoy, how that burns us up."--Engineer Section, Aase Headquarters, A.P.O. 600.

"It sure is strange how promotions are held up in this outfit. Even doesn't have the rank for his job. Well it's 'nice' that all the 'gallant' warriors back home can set back and tell about how much fighting they could do and get promoted for it while the boys over here do the job without reward."--39th Field Artillery Bn.

"A person could starve to death if they wait on the allotment, I sure am glad I sent the fifty and the fifteen dollar allotment it will help a little until the other allotment comes. I don't like the idea about going to the Red Cross but go to them if you have to don't let things get too bad."- -Ho. Co. 2nd An. 531st Engineer Regiment.

"You all knew that I arrived safely. But there are lot of wives of officers and men who did not know the fate of their husbands until Feb. 23 when the first letters from us began to reach home. As I told you before, even our Colonel was wild about the way they handled our safe arrival cards."- -Second Air Defense Wing. hhh l8.

General. In general, the morale of the troops continues to be excellent. Frequent mention is made about the excellent fighting quality of the German soldier, but in each case the writers express confidence in their ability to beat them. Troops that have seen action and were wounded, frequently express a desire to get back into action with their units. The following comments were observed during the past week:

"I'm afraid it's too much to hope for that the Axis have 'fired their big gun' for they are still a tough bunch. I am more than confident that we will take them and beat them so bad that they will never want to fight anymore, but it is going to take some hard punching on our side to do yet. To slack up for a minute would be about the very worst thing we could possibly do just at this particular time."--Hq., 12th Fighter Command.

"One thing that I can say though and this that the Germans certainly won't be a pushover by an means. All our soldiers have the greatest respect for them as soldiers and fighters. One thing that should be of interest to you is that they are respecting the Geneva pact very rigidly as far as the medical corps is concerned. They never do anything to even the front line hospitals and anything marked with a red cross is left strictly alone. There have been reports come back to us of doctors having been captured, in which case they are stripped of their equipment and are then shown the way home and told to cram."--21st General Hospital.

"The other day I went to one of our boys in the Base hospital. He is in bad shape. No doubt they will send him home. For he is no longer fit for duty, and I am afraid he is not much good at home either. But his spirit is still high and he wants to stay and go back for duty. He and many others are in the same boat and feel the same. With boys like that it won't be long now and we shall be on our way back home."--3rd Service Gp.

"The boys came back fighting mad....They did a terrific job and aim to keep it up. You've been to the fights and seen a plucky kid take one on the chin when his guard was down. How you cheered him when he got up and turned the tide of battle, well that's the way we feel."--Base Hq., A.P.O. 600.

"Our men are wonderful. Bravery is lust a part of their daily duty. They give all they have, live under tough conditions, such as sleeping on the cold wet ground, slim rations, dirty bodies, and you never have a complaint. I never saw anything like it. when the wounded come in they just keep still until they are cared for, and boy oh: do they appreciate the care you give them. I had one fellow call me to his bed three times after I had finished with him to thank me. I thought what I have done is nothing compared to the stuff you boys are going through. Guts is the word for it. The air pilots just go up after those German sons of B's like cats after rats. Regardless of odds they take with them and stay as long as the plane holds together or the sky is clear of Messerschmitts. The para troopers and tankmen, well all of them are afraid of no one. Keep up the good work at home. Equipment is what we want. Just give these boys plenty of planes and tanks and they will do the job for you."--52nd Station Hospital.

"The way how the wounded fellows behave is really wonderful. These fellows who really have gone through everything up at the front, keep their chins up, never talk about themselves, and seem to be filled with a quiet determination that's really wonderful.The only time when a person may hear them speak of themselves is wrung out of them only when the pair becomes unendurable."--261st Med. Amph. Bn.

"You say the papers back home are full of the stories about the 8th Army. It is a wonderful Army. I can say now that we are doing our part also and not so very far away from that 8th Army. You can bet if the weather ever gives us a break the damn Hun isn't going to last long in Africa. Then maybe I hope we get a little rest and then destroy him on the European fields. Don't worry Mother we will give him hell when the time comes. It has been proven they are not so damn good when they meet up with the right men and equipment. Your old Uncle Sam has the equipment and men with guts. We are also fighting for the right thing Freedom and Christianity. They are not, they can't win. It will take a little time but Mother I hope it won't be long."--Company B, 751st Tank An. (M).
Captain, F A. , Chief Aase Censor.
59 Inclosures:
58 Forms 912, Serial Nos.
BCD 1-1 to BCD 1-19, inclusive, 2 copies each.
Statistical Report in triplicate
DISTRIBUTION:
3 A.C. of S., G-2 War Department (thru channels)
1 Commanding General, Fifth Army
1 Commanding General, XII Air Force
1 Commanding Officer, MBS
File
